content:
5 min read
Just now

--

A walk through the MCP demo that finally made it click.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

AI-generated image by gpt-image-2A newsletter is more work than it looks. Someone has to research the topic. Someone has to draft it in a voice people will actually read. And someone has to lay it out so it doesn’t land as a grey wall of text. Traditionally that’s a few people, or one person bouncing between a browser, a writing app, and a design tool for an afternoon.
I watched someone produce the whole thing, research to formatted draft, from a single chat window. No team. A handful of plain-English instructions. And the only thing in the entire exercise that resembled code was a short block of configuration pasted once and never touched again.
The demo comes from CampusX, Nitish Singh’s channel, as the opener to his series on the Model Context Protocol. It’s the clearest answer I’ve seen to a fair question a lot of people are quietly asking: fine, MCP connects AI to tools, but what does that actually let me do?
This is what it lets you do.
What actually happened
Start with the chat app you type into, Claude Desktop in this case. On its own, it can write you a lovely draft and nothing else. It can’t search the live web. It can’t save a file to your machine. It can’t lay anything out. It’s the brilliant-and-blind problem I wrote about in the last piece: clever, and sealed inside a box.
MCP is what opens the box. The demo connected a few ready-made MCP servers. The cleanest way to picture a server is as an adapter that hands the AI exactly one new ability: one adapter lets it search the web, another lets it read and write files, another handles turning plain text into something laid-out and visual.
The important part: none of these were built for the demo. They already exist, off the shelf, built by other people and shared openly. There are already thousands of these ready-made servers available publicly, the way open-source code is, one for nearly any common tool you can think of. Connecting them meant pasting a short configuration, a few lines telling the app which servers to load, one time.
That configuration is the only “code” in the whole thing, and calling it code is generous. It’s closer to a list. Everything after that is conversation.
Three prompts, three phases
From there the build moved in three plain-English steps, each one a phase of the actual work:
Research. A prompt asking it to go find what’s worth saying on the topic. The AI reached for the web-search adapter and pulled current material on its own. No opening tabs, no copying links into the chat.
Write. A prompt asking it to turn that research into a draft in the newsletter’s voice. It used the file adapter to write the draft out as a real file, not just print it back in the chat window.
Design. A prompt asking it to format the draft into something presentable. The layout adapter turned plain text into a finished-looking piece.
Laid out as a flow, the pattern is the whole lesson:
Phase The plain-English ask The ability the AI reached for Research “find what matters on this topic” a web-search server Write “draft it in this voice” a file read/write server Design “make it look presentable” a layout / visual server
Three asks. Three phases. One window. The person set direction at each step; the AI did the legwork by reaching for whichever connected ability that step happened to need.
Why this isn’t just “ChatGPT can write my newsletter”
This is the distinction that matters, because it’s the one most people miss.
A chatbot on its own can draft text. That part has been true for a while. What it can’t do alone is everything around the writing: getting current facts instead of stale ones, saving the result somewhere useful, making it look right. Those were exactly the steps that kept dragging you back into the loop. Open the browser, copy the findings into a document, move the document into a design tool, fix the formatting by hand. Every one of those handoffs was a human. Usually you.
MCP removes the handoffs. The research tool, the writing tool, and the design tool are all reachable by the AI inside one conversation, because they all speak the same language it does. You stop being the relay runner carrying work between apps. That is the entire trick, and it’s why “three prompts” can stand in for an afternoon of tab-switching.
The honest part
Watching a smooth demo, it’s tempting to conclude the work vanished. It didn’t. It moved, and it’s worth being clear-eyed about where it went.
The output is only ever as good as the servers you plug in and the direction you give. A web-search server can surface something outdated, or plain wrong, and the AI won’t always catch it. The draft still needs a person who knows the subject to spot what’s off and cut what’s filler. “No code” is genuinely true. “No judgment” is not, and anyone selling you the second one is selling you a future correction.
And the quiet cost, again. Every server you connect can see things and do things on your behalf. A file server can write files. A web server reaches out to the open internet. Convenience and access are two sides of one coin, and that tradeoff deserves its own careful look another time. For a newsletter demo it’s harmless. For your real accounts and your real data, it’s worth a pause before you wire everything together.
The shift worth noticing
A year ago, getting an AI to run this workflow would have meant a developer building custom connectors for each tool by hand, one for search, one for files, one for design, each maintained separately. A big part of why one person can now assemble it in an afternoon is that those connectors stopped being everyone’s private problem and became a shared standard. That standard is MCP, which as of December 2025 is an open protocol stewarded by the Linux Foundation and backed by most of the major AI companies. The plumbing got built once, by the people who make the tools, so the rest of us don’t have to.
What’s left for you is the part that was always the actual point: deciding what you want, and saying it clearly enough that something can act on it.
The newsletter is a deliberately small example. Swap in the tools you already use and the same pattern holds: research, act, produce, driven by description instead of code. The barrier to automating a real chunk of your work used to be “can you build the integration.” It’s quietly turning into “can you describe the outcome.”
That’s a very different question. And a lot more people can answer it than could write the code.
